figures.caption: Figure 3: Results of the ablation study under the medium-anchor regime across 3 datasets (weighted by image count). Results are shown for DA3 and MiDaS, comparing the full model with a variant where F (x) = 0 as the number of basis maps (x-axis) increases. Left: AbsRel (↓). Right: δ1 (↑). Solid lines denote the full model; dotted lines denote the F (x) = 0 variant.

Figure 2: Example from SUN RGB-D [21] with DA3 [4]. Left to right: scale field, intermediate representation, and MDE depth. Their spatial correspondence highlights how semantic and geometric cues in the MDE outputs relate to scale variation.
Table 1: KITTI results with different anchor numbers. Low/Med/High correspond to 10–15 / 100–120 / 500–530 anchors per image. Best results are in bold, second-best are underlined.

Table 2: Weighted-average results over UrbanSyn and SUN RGB-D (weighted by image count). Low/Med/High correspond to 10–15 / 100–120 / 500–530 anchors per image. Best results are in bold, second-best are underlined.

Figure 1: Overview of our approach. A pretrained monocular depth estimation (MDE) model produces an initial depth estimation DMDE(x) and intermediate representations F (x). The basis map generator takes these outputs and produces a K-dimensional embedding E(x) = [E1(x), . . . , EK(x)], whose components define K basis maps. Sparse metric anchors are used to weight these maps via a least-squares problem to obtain a spatially varying scale field.
